narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 64% chance that you would not pass. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students with stronger projected outcomes. The model's decision hinged most heavily on three key aspects of your profile: your planned part-time enrollment status, your Year 3 academic decile ranking, and your Year 1 decile ranking. Above all, choosing to attend part-time stood out as the strongest driver pushing the prediction toward failure—unlike the vast majority of students who passed the bar (over 93%), who enrolled full-time, this choice significantly raised concerns about your overall readiness and success trajectory. On a more positive note, your solid Year 3 decile of 7—better than the average of about 5.9 for those who passed—worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success and highlighting your later academic improvement. However, your Year 1 decile of 4, which is lower than the typical 6 for bar passers, countered that by nudging the outlook toward failure, suggesting an early stumble that the model weighed heavily. Your LSAT score of 37 and undergraduate GPA of 3.20 were close to averages for successful students, while your middle family income bracket aligned with over a third of bar passers, so they played neutral roles here. Compared to typical accepted applicants—who often combine full-time commitment with consistently high deciles from the start—your profile showed promise in recovery but was offset by the part-time plan and initial grades. This isn't a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven flag based on patterns from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider reapplying with a full-time enrollment plan, as that alone could dramatically shift your predicted odds; bolstering early-grade equivalents through additional coursework or retakes might also help. You could also retake the LSAT for a slight edge or seek programs with flexible tracks proven to support part-time success. Keep building on that Year 3 momentum—many students turn things around, and with targeted adjustments, you'll strengthen your case next time.
